elements.sent-2411-001: Building a high-performance JIT-capable VM for a dynamic language has traditionally required a tremendous amount of time, money, and expertise.
elements.sent-2411-002: We present Deegen, a meta-compiler that allows users to generate a high-performance JIT-capable VM for their own language at an engineering cost similar to writing a simple interpreter.
elements.sent-2411-003: Deegen takes in the execution semantics of the bytecodes implemented as C++ functions, and automatically generates a two-tier VM execution engine with a state-of-the-art interpreter, a state-of-the-art baseline JIT, and the tier-switching logic that connects them into a self-adaptive system.
elements.sent-2411-004: We are the first to demonstrate the automatic generation of a JIT compiler, and the automatic generation of an interpreter that outperforms the state of the art.
elements.sent-2411-005: Our performance comes from a long list of optimizations supported by Deegen, including bytecode specialization and quickening, register pinning, tag register optimization, call inline caching, generic inline caching, JIT polymorphic IC, JIT IC inline slab, type-check removal and strength reduction, type-based slow-path extraction and outlining, JIT hot-cold code splitting, and JIT OSR-entry.
elements.sent-2411-006: These optimizations are either employed automatically, or guided by the language implementer through intuitive APIs.
elements.sent-2411-007: As a result, the disassembly of the Deegen-generated interpreter, baseline JIT, and the generated JIT code rivals the assembly code hand-written by assembly experts in state-of-the-art VMs.
elements.sent-2411-008: We implement LuaJIT Remake (LJR), a standard-compliant Lua 5.1 VM, using Deegen.
elements.sent-2411-009: Across 44 benchmarks, LJR's interpreter is on average 179\% faster than the official PUC Lua interpreter, and 31\% faster than LuaJIT's interpreter.
elements.sent-2411-010: LJR's baseline JIT has negligible startup delay, and its execution performance is on average 360\% faster than PUC Lua and only 33\% slower (but faster on 13/44 benchmarks) than LuaJIT's optimizing JIT.
elements.sent-2411-011: Dynamic languages are widely used for their productivity.
elements.sent-2411-012: But they are also known for being slow because they cannot be statically compiled to efficient native code.
elements.sent-2411-013: Just-In-Time (JIT) compilers are used to improve their performance through profile-guided speculative compilation at runtime, but building a good JIT-capable Virtual Machine (VM) is notoriously difficult.
elements.sent-2411-014: For example, every JavaScript VM in modern browsers is built through centuries of engineering time from some of the best compiler engineers in the world, with a huge build cost estimated at \$225M~\cite{chrisCumminsThesis for V8~\cite{v8jit and $\sim$\$50M~\cite{boehm2009cocomo for JSC~\cite{applejsc and SM~\cite{spidermonkey .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-015: The high cost has its reason: to yield good performance on both short-running and long-running workloads, the VM needs an optimized interpreter and multiple tiers of JIT compilers to balance startup delay (the time to generate the JIT code) and execution performance~\cite{speculationinjsc, v8turbofan, v8sparkplug, spidermonkeyDesign .\footnote{This paper assumes that readers are familiar with modern multi-tier VM architectures.}}
elements.sent-2411-016: For readers seeking an introduction to the topic, we recommend reading the section \href{https://webkit.org/blog/10308/speculation-in-javascriptcore/\#:~:text=The\%20tiers\%20of\%20JavaScriptCore.}
elements.sent-2411-017: For best performance, the interpreter needs to be written in assembly~\cite{v8ignition,jscAsmInterpreter,luajitAsmInterpreter , as producing a state-of-the-art interpreter from a high-level language like C is beyond the capability of general-purpose compilers~\cite{luajitAsmInterpreterMotivation .}}
elements.sent-2411-018: In a JIT compiler, the logic in the generated JIT code is hand-crafted by programmers directly in assembly instructions, which differ for each JIT tier, or even each hardware ISA.
elements.sent-2411-019: For example, V8 once had nine baseline JIT implementations for different ISAs~\cite{10.1145/3033019.3033025 .}
elements.sent-2411-020: Domain-specific dynamic language optimizations, such as hidden classes~\cite{10.1145/74877.74884 , inline caching~\cite{10.5555/646149.679193 , type speculation~\cite{10.1145/191080.191116 , and watchpoints~\cite{10.1145/2542142.2542143 are required to get good performance, but they require high expertise to get right.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-021: Furthermore, the lower VM tiers must tier up to the higher tiers when the code gets hot, and in the case of a speculation failure or watchpoint invalidation, the higher tiers must exit to the lower tiers using on-stack replacement (known as an OSR-exit or deoptimization)~\cite{10.1145/143095.143114 .}
elements.sent-2411-022: All of these complications make it notoriously difficult to write a modern dynamic language VM with a multi-tier JIT.
elements.sent-2411-023: Such engineering complexity is unaffordable for most VM developer groups.
elements.sent-2411-024: Repurposing a VM from another language is also not an option, as any small difference in the language semantics can become a big change in the assembly implementation, thus a sweeping change to the interpreter and the JIT compilers.
elements.sent-2411-025: As a result, developers have to fall back to simpler designs with worse performance.
elements.sent-2411-026: Many VMs do not have a JIT at all.
elements.sent-2411-027: And for those with a JIT, the design is almost always single-tiered for its relative simplicity, even though the lack of a baseline JIT can cause significant performance degration on real-world large programs~\cite{hhvmCgo21 .}
elements.sent-2411-028: Some (e.g., PyPy~\cite{pypy and LuaJIT~\cite{luajit ) employ tracing JIT instead of method JIT, which simplifies engineering but has uneven (workload-dependent) and less predictable performance~\cite{mozzilaAbandonTracingJit,convergemetatracing .}}}
elements.sent-2411-029: And some (e.g., the PHP 8 JIT~\cite{engebreth2021php ) are primarily optimized for long-running numerical workloads, which are less sensitive to startup delay and easier to optimize.}
elements.sent-2411-030: However, such a JIT has little benefit for most real-world uses of dynamic languages~\cite{phpWordpressPerf .}
elements.sent-2411-031: To reduce engineering cost, Truffle~\cite{trufflePaper pioneered the idea of decoupling the JIT compiler from the language: Truffle implements a generic JIT that generates code by partial evaluation~\cite{partialevaluationbook of a guest language AST interpreter on the input AST program at runtime, also known as the first Futamura projection~\cite{futamuraPaper .}}}
elements.sent-2411-032: While Truffle achieves remarkably high JIT peak throughput, the approach has fundamental limitations.
elements.sent-2411-033: Since the expensive partial evaluation happens at runtime, the startup delay of the Truffle JIT is significantly higher than conventional JIT compilers~\cite{truffleRubyStartupDelay, truffleSeminarTalk .}
elements.sent-2411-034: The Truffle interpreter is designed to facilitate partial evaluation, but this comes at the cost of performance and memory: \citet{Marr2022GraalWorkshop showed that Truffle's Python and Ruby interpreters were $\sim$10x slower than CPython and CRuby while consuming more memory as of 2022.\footnote{The Truffle developers have informed us that Truffle's interpreter performance has improved since then.}}
elements.sent-2411-035: As a result, for many real-world use cases where the startup delay matters (e.g., client apps, and server apps with continuous deployment), Truffle's performance is unsatisfactory~\cite{truffleRubyStartupDelay .}
elements.sent-2411-036: Finally, Truffle relies on Oracle Java, so languages built upon it are bound to the Oracle Java ecosystem, as they cannot run on a standard JRE.

elements.sent-2411-037: To overcome these limitations, we present Deegen, a fundamentally new approach to building high-performance VMs at low engineering cost.
elements.sent-2411-038: Deegen is a compiler generator that automatically generates a VM execution engine from C++ bytecode semantics at build time (\figref{fig:overview ).}
elements.sent-2411-039: In Futamura's taxonomy, Deegen is a second Futamura projector.\footnote{In the sense that Deegen statically generates a compiler.}
elements.sent-2411-040: However, our internal design is different from Futamura's proposal.
elements.sent-2411-041: Specifically, the user describes the execution semantics of each bytecode in C++ inside our framework.
elements.sent-2411-042: The description is compiled to LLVM IR~\cite{lattner2004 using the unmodified Clang compiler.}
elements.sent-2411-043: Deegen then performs analysis, transformation, and optimization at the LLVM IR level to automatically generate the whole execution engine.
elements.sent-2411-044: The generated JIT compiler generates code using Copy-and-Patch~\cite{copyandpatch , a light-weight and fast code generation technique that works by composing and configuring pre-built stencils.}
elements.sent-2411-045: Thus, the generated system is self-contained, and does not need LLVM at runtime.
elements.sent-2411-046: Deegen currently automatically generates a VM with a state-of-the-art interpreter, a state-of-the-art baseline JIT, and the tier-switching logic that connects the two tiers, which will be the topic of this paper.
elements.sent-2411-047: We already have a concrete design and a partial implementation to generate the third-tier optimizing JIT as well, but we leave it to a future paper.
elements.sent-2411-048: We are the first to demonstrate:.
elements.sent-2411-049: A practical compiler-compiler that works for an industrial-strength dynamic language (Lua).
elements.sent-2411-050: The automatic generation of a two-tier JIT-capable VM and the associated profiling, tier-up and OSR-entry logic that connects the tiers into a self-adaptive system.
elements.sent-2411-051: Furthermore, everything is generated from a single source of truth (C++ bytecode semantics).
elements.sent-2411-052: The automatic generation of a state-of-the-art interpreter whose code quality can match or surpass existing state-of-the-art interpreters hand-coded in assembly by experts.
elements.sent-2411-053: The automatic generation of a state-of-the-art baseline JIT compiler that has a negligible startup delay and generates high quality machine code that rivals existing state-of-the-art baseline JITs.
elements.sent-2411-054: This is achieved by an improved Copy-and-Patch technique that transparently supports polymorphic inline caching, hot-cold code splitting, and other optimizations.
elements.sent-2411-055: The design of a bytecode semantic description framework that facilitates build-time analysis, transformation, and optimization of the bytecode semantics; allows easy expression of common dynamic language optimizations (e.g., logic specialization, inline caching, and type speculation); and features an intuitive, flexible, and user-friendly interface.
elements.sent-2411-056: To evaluate Deegen, we use it to implement LuaJIT Remake (LJR)\footnote{Deegen and LuaJIT Remake are available under the Apache 2.0 license at \url{https://github.com/luajit-remake/luajit-remake .}}
elements.sent-2411-057: To keep our research focused, however, we did not implement garbage collection\footnote{To fairly compare to LuaJIT and PUC Lua, we also turned off their garbage collection in benchmarks.}
elements.sent-2411-058: Across 44 benchmarks, LJR's interpreter is on average 179\% faster than the official PUC Lua interpreter~\cite{puclua51 , and 31\% faster than the interpreter in LuaJIT 2.1~\cite{luajit21 .}}
elements.sent-2411-059: LJR's baseline JIT has negligible startup delay, and its execution performance is on average 360\% faster than PUC Lua, and 33\% slower than LuaJIT's optimizing JIT.
elements.sent-2411-060: Despite that a baseline JIT is by design not intended to compete with an optimizing JIT on peak throughput, we are already faster than LuaJIT on 13/44 benchmarks.
elements.sent-2411-061: We believe the performance gap with LuaJIT in peak JIT throughput will be closed once our optimizing JIT lands.
elements.sent-2411-062: The goal of this paper is to describe Deegen's architecture and design, and shed light on the key ideas, tricks, and observations that made Deegen possible.

elements.sent-2411-082: The user is responsible for implementing the parser that builds up the bytecode stream from the program source.
elements.sent-2411-083: For this purpose, Deegen generates a rich set of bytecode-builder APIs from the bytecode semantics as a C++ header file.
elements.sent-2411-084: As one can see, the API hides all details about the internal bytecode representation and variant selection, and is type-safe and robust by design.
elements.sent-2411-085: For example, it is impossible to get the argument order wrong, to forget to provide an operand, or to supply a value of a bad type.
elements.sent-2411-086: Deegen automatically generates a two-tier VM execution engine, with its high-level architecture illustrated in \figref{fig:overview .}
elements.sent-2411-087: At VM runtime, the user-written parser translates each source program function to a bytecode sequence by calling the Deegen-generated bytecode builder APIs.
elements.sent-2411-088: The bytecode sequence is then executed by the interpreter.
elements.sent-2411-089: Deegen automatically emits profiling logic in the generated interpreter to detect hot functions.
elements.sent-2411-090: These functions are then tiered up to the baseline JIT for higher execution throughput, at the cost of a negligible startup delay and the memory to hold the JIT code.
elements.sent-2411-091: With future work, execution can further tier up to a speculative optimizing JIT.
elements.sent-2411-092: The rest of this section provides an overview of each component of the generated system.
elements.sent-2411-094: Each bytecode is implemented by a native function and, at the end of the function, control is transferred directly to the next bytecode (aka., direct-threading~\cite{10.1145/362248.362270 ) via a tail call (a jmp instruction).}
elements.sent-2411-095: Deegen supports many optimizations to improve performance, including bytecode specialization, dynamic quickening~\cite{10.1145/1899661.1869633 , register pinning~\cite{ghcRegisterPinning , tag registers~\cite{jscTagRegisterOptimization , monomorphic inline caching~\cite{speculationinjsc , and slow-path outlining~\cite{luajitAsmInterpreterMotivation .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-096: The primary goal of the baseline JIT is to compile as quickly as possible.
elements.sent-2411-097: Generating good code is certainly desirable, but a secondary priority.
elements.sent-2411-098: To this end, the baseline JIT generated by Deegen lowers a bytecode stream directly to machine code, without going through any analyses or IRs, as illustrated in \figref{fig:baseline-jit-compilation-pipeline .}
elements.sent-2411-099: Since compilation speed is the top concern, inline caching (IC) is the only high-level optimization: the baseline JIT employs polymorphic IC~\cite{10.5555/646149.679193 based on self-modifying code.}
elements.sent-2411-100: Nevertheless, many low-level optimizations are applied at build time to improve the machine code, including binding-time analysis~\cite{jones1988automatic to identify runtime constants that can be burnt into the instructions, IC inline slab~\cite{javascriptcoreInlineSlab to eliminate branches for monomorphic ICs, hot-cold splitting~\cite{javascriptcoreHotColdSplitting for better code locality, and jump-to-fallthrough transform to eliminate unnecessary jumps.}}}
elements.sent-2411-101: Through an improved Copy-and-Patch~\cite{copyandpatch technique, we are able to employ these optimizations automatically and without compromising compilation speed.}
elements.sent-2411-102: Inline caching (IC) is a critical optimization for all VM tiers.
elements.sent-2411-103: Deegen has two~IC mechanisms: call IC and generic IC.
elements.sent-2411-104: The call IC optimizes guest language function calls: Deegen automatically applies direct/closure dual-mode call IC~\cite{javascriptcoreCallIc .}
elements.sent-2411-105: The generic IC (\secref{sec:generic-inline-caching ) is a set of APIs that allows users to express arbitrary IC semantics (for example, to optimize object access).}
elements.sent-2411-106: Deegen lowers the IC semantics to highly optimized implementations that are customized to fit the different needs and constraints of each VM tier.
elements.sent-2411-107: The interpreter uses monomorphic IC with dynamic quickening, since the dispatch overhead in polymorphic IC is high without code generation.
elements.sent-2411-108: The baseline JIT, on the other hand, uses polymorphic IC (\figref{fig:polymorphic-ic ), and leverages the capability of JIT code and self-modifying code for maximum performance.}
elements.sent-2411-109: This is another prime example where Deegen lowers a single source of truth (the IC semantics) in different ways to best fit each use case.
elements.sent-2411-110: Polymorphic IC~\cite{10.5555/646149.679193 is a powerful optimization for the JIT compiler.}
elements.sent-2411-111: The most efficient polymorphic IC employs generation of new JIT code and self-modification of existing JIT code on the fly, as illustrated in \figref{fig:polymorphic-ic .}
elements.sent-2411-112: In a hand-written VM, this is achieved by carefully planning all the logic pieces (the different IC cases, the support logic, etc.) in assembly, and then hand-craft the IC compiler that emits and patches those logic pieces in machine code.
elements.sent-2411-113: In Deegen, however, all the implementations must be generated automatically from the IC semantics in C++ code.
elements.sent-2411-114: Clearly, this is a challenging task: at the minimum, we need to lower the C++ semantics to a piece of machine code that can be dynamically patched to append new logic pieces, and LLVM is simply not designed for this.
elements.sent-2411-115: As it turns out, the core trick is to repurpose a little-known LLVM IR node called \code{CallBr to trick LLVM into doing the heavy lifting, and use assembly transforms to finish the remaining bits.}
elements.sent-2411-116: The VM must detect hot functions so they can be compiled by the JIT compiler.
elements.sent-2411-117: Deegen accomplishes this by automatically emitting profiling logic into the generated interpreter to compute the number of bytecodes executed in each function.
elements.sent-2411-118: Specifically, Deegen generates appropriate accounting logic when a branch is taken and when the function exits (either normally or due to an exception).
elements.sent-2411-119: This gives us accurate profiling information at minimal performance overhead.
elements.sent-2411-120: The interpreter can enter the baseline JIT at function entries (called a tier-up) or at customizable points inside the function body (called an OSR-entry or a hot-loop transfer).
elements.sent-2411-122: Deegen transparently injects profiling logic to detect hot functions.
elements.sent-2411-123: When a function reaches a hotness threshold, compilation is triggered and future calls to the function will use the optimized version.
elements.sent-2411-124: Tier-up can only elevate future calls, but for long-running functions, we also want to elevate the current invocation: this is supported by OSR-entry.
elements.sent-2411-125: OSR-entry requires simple user input: the user specifies the bytecode kinds that are worth checking for OSR-entry (usually the loop jumps).
elements.sent-2411-126: Deegen will generate OSR-entry checks for these bytecodes.
elements.sent-2411-127: Then, if the current-running function has met the hotness criteria, it will trigger compilation as soon as one of these bytecodes is executed, and OSR-enter the JIT code from that bytecode.
elements.sent-2411-128: Deegen provides a simple set of APIs for built-in library functions in C/C++ to call guest language functions and to throw exceptions (\secref{appendix:cpp-library-definition-apis ).}
elements.sent-2411-129: Providing richer APIs with more control, e.g., stack introspection, is merely engineering and left as future work.
elements.sent-2411-130: Deegen is agnostic of the guest language syntax and the user is required to implement the parser that translates the source program to bytecodes.
elements.sent-2411-131: For this purpose, Deegen generates flexible, user-friendly and robust bytecode builder APIs (see \figref{fig:bytecode-builder-api and \secref{appendix:bytecode-builder-apis ), with advanced features such as inspecting and replacing bytecodes.}}
elements.sent-2411-132: The APIs are generated as a C++ header file, and fully work for C++ IDE features such as syntax highlighting and autocompletion.
elements.sent-2411-133: Deegen has ambitious goals, but to keep our research focused and reasonably scoped, several areas are left as future work.
elements.sent-2411-134: First, Deegen is agnostic of garbage collection (GC).
elements.sent-2411-135: Specifically, the inline caching (IC) entities generated by Deegen may reference GC-tracked objects, so to make Deegen production-ready, we would need to provide either an integrated GC or a way for the user-written GC to track the IC entities.
elements.sent-2411-136: Second, we do not consider compatibility of C-bindings with reference implementations: supporting efficient C-binding with backward-compatibility has been an active research area on its own~\cite{hpy,sulong,cinder,trufflerubycapi .}
elements.sent-2411-137: Third, Deegen only targets the x86-64 ISA.
elements.sent-2411-138: While Deegen offloads most heavy lifting of code generation to LLVM at build time, portability still requires non-trivial work due to our lack of access and experience with other ISAs.
elements.sent-2411-139: Finally, Deegen does not support concurrent compilation, so we do not have to think about race conditions between the JIT compiler and the application code.
elements.section-2411-004: The Bytecode Semantic Description Framework Design
elements.sent-2411-140: The most important design decision about our bytecode semantic description framework is to decide what concepts it should understand.
elements.sent-2411-141: As \citet{ricetheorem proved, all non-trivial semantic properties of programs are undecidable, so the knowledge we can extract from generic C++ code is fundamentally limited.}
elements.sent-2411-142: We must have domain-specific specialized models to support domain-specific optimizations.
elements.sent-2411-143: However, this inevitably hurts generality and penalizes use cases that fall outside the model, so finding the balance is critical.
elements.sent-2411-144: This section discusses our current model and design choices.
elements.sent-2411-145: We recognize that as an initial proof of concept, our model is far from optimal in both generality and performance.
elements.sent-2411-146: Nevertheless, we leave further design space exploration as future work: it requires a lot of engineering and experimentation, but is orthogonal to the core ideas presented in this paper.
elements.sent-2411-147: We assume a register-based bytecode-machine model where each virtual register (i.e., stack slot) stores an 8-byte boxed value.
elements.sent-2411-148: Each bytecode operand may be a virtual register in the stack frame, a constant boxed value in the constant table, an integer literal value, or a consecutive range of virtual registers in the stack frame.
elements.sent-2411-149: We recognize that stack-based bytecode can be more compact, but we leave supporting it as future work.
elements.sent-2411-150: We made this decision because the bytecode semantics is the single source of truth in Deegen, and supporting multiple formats of bytecode semantics would add significant engineering complexity with limited research novelty.
elements.sent-2411-151: Similarly, we currently do not support 4-byte or 16-byte boxing, so we can focus on the research novelty.
elements.sent-2411-152: The boxing scheme~\cite{Gudeman93representingtype is fundamental to every dynamic language VM.}
elements.sent-2411-153: Many schemes with different tradeoffs have been used in practice, such as SMI-boxing~\cite{v8smiboxing , NaN-boxing~\cite{luajitNaNBoxing , NuN-boxing~\cite{spidermonkeyNunboxing , and trivial boxing.}}}
elements.sent-2411-154: Therefore, Deegen does not hardcode a particular scheme, but provides APIs for users to describe it, so that Deegen can reason about the scheme and automatically apply relevant optimizations (\secref{sec:type-based-optimization ).}
elements.sent-2411-155: The control flow transfer between bytecodes needs to be implemented differently in each VM tier, and the ability to understand the CFG is important for the future optimizing JIT.
elements.sent-2411-156: Thus, users must use Deegen APIs to transfer control between bytecodes (e.g., see \figref{fig:bytecode-add-example ).}
elements.sent-2411-157: These constructs should be sufficient for a wide range of languages.
elements.sent-2411-158: As just explained, calls to guest language functions must be done using Deegen APIs (e.g., \deegenKeyword{MakeCall , \deegenKeyword{MakeTailCall , etc.).}}
elements.sent-2411-159: Additionally, the logic after the call must be written in continuation-passing style (CPS) as a separate function (see \figref{fig:bytecode-add-example ).}
elements.sent-2411-161: First, they hide the complicated implementation details of the call (such as variadic arguments handling) from the user, and lets Deegen automatically generate optimized code behind the scenes.
elements.sent-2411-162: Second, CPS makes it easier to support speculative inlining and stackful coroutines.
elements.sent-2411-163: Finally, being able to understand calls allows important high-level optimizations (e.g., call inline caching and speculative inlining) to happen automatically and transparently.
elements.sent-2411-164: Of course, the downside is that if a language requires a call concept not supported by Deegen, it would have to be soft-implemented using existing mechanisms, which hurts performance.
elements.sent-2411-165: To avoid this as much as possible, Deegen provides built-in support for variadic arguments, variadic calls, proper tail calls, in-place calls, and functions returning multiple or variadic results, which we believe is sufficient for a wide range of languages.
elements.sent-2411-166: Deegen can also be extended to support new call concepts as needed.
elements.sent-2411-167: Many languages support first-class functions with lexical capturing.
elements.sent-2411-168: We require users to implement lexical captures using the Deegen-provided upvalue mechanism, which is based on Lua's upvalue~\cite{luaUpvalue implementation.\footnote{While the word ``upvalue'' is Lua jargon, the upvalue mechanism (also called flat closures~\cite{10.1145/800055.802037 ) is a language-neutral method of implementing first-class lexical-scoped functions that can be used for any language.}}}
elements.sent-2411-169: We made this requirement as the future optimizing JIT must know which virtual registers are captured to reason about the data flow of a function.
elements.sent-2411-170: Nevertheless, supporting alternate schemes with different performance/memory tradeoffs (e.g., JSC's scope chain mechanism) is completely possible and left as future work.
elements.sent-2411-171: The execution semantics of a bytecode consists of multiple components.
elements.sent-2411-172: We have seen the main component and the return continuation in \figref{fig:bytecode-add-example .}
elements.sent-2411-173: The third kind, the slow path, will be covered in the next paragraph.
elements.sent-2411-174: There is no restriction on how control flow transfers between components: for example, return continuations can make further calls and potentially recurse.
elements.sent-2411-175: The main component is special in that it supports inline caching.
elements.sent-2411-176: Call IC (\secref{sec:generated-vm-architecture ) is automatically employed for calls made by the main component.}
elements.sent-2411-177: Users may also employ generic IC, which we will describe later in the section.
elements.sent-2411-178: Slow paths are pervasive in dynamic languages: for example, many languages have complex rules to handle an add of two non-numbers or a call to a non-function.

elements.sent-2411-347: The rest of this section elaborates on each of the components.
elements.sent-2411-348: As explained in \secref{sec:semantic-description-framework , the AOT slow paths come from a bytecode's slow path components and their associated return continuations.}
elements.sent-2411-349: Their logic in the baseline JIT tier is very similar to their interpreter counterparts, except that control must be transferred back to the JIT code at the end.
elements.sent-2411-350: This requires information about the JIT code, which is not available in the original bytecode.
elements.sent-2411-351: Deegen solves this problem by letting the baseline JIT generate a SlowPathData stream that contains all information about the bytecode and the JIT code needed by the AOT slow paths.
elements.sent-2411-352: The baseline JIT compilation happens in four steps (\figref{fig:baseline-jit-compilation-pipeline ): compute total code and data sizes, allocate memory, generate everything, and fix branch targets.}
elements.sent-2411-353: In the generation step, we scan through the bytecode once, and generate code and data for each bytecode using the Copy-and-Patch~\cite{copyandpatch technique.}
elements.sent-2411-354: As one can see, Copy-and-Patch allows us to generate code by literally copying and patching, and the logic is always branchless by design, so a modern CPU can leverage its ILP to the utmost.
elements.sent-2411-355: The code also employs continuation-passing and register-pinning: the same techniques that made our interpreter fast.
elements.sent-2411-356: With all of these, we are able to achieve end-to-end compilation throughputs measured in GB/s, which to our knowledge, is unprecedented.
elements.sent-2411-357: Polymorphic IC is the most important optimization in the baseline JIT.
elements.sent-2411-358: It is also the only high-level optimization,\footnote{This is the typical design choice used by state-of-the-art baseline JITs, e.g., those in JavaScriptCore and SpiderMonkey.}
elements.sent-2411-359: The JIT code contains a self-modifying code (SMC) region, which initially only contains a jump to the IC miss slow path (the $\lambda_i$ logic).
elements.sent-2411-360: At execution time, new IC stubs (the $\lambda_e$ logic) are created by the IC miss slow path.
elements.sent-2411-361: Each IC stub is a piece of JIT code (see \figref{fig:ic-entry-baseline-jit-assembly ) that checks whether $IC_{key}$ hits, and branches to the next IC stub on miss.}
elements.sent-2411-362: If the IC stub is small enough, and the SMC region is not already holding an IC stub, the IC stub can sit in the SMC region: this is called the inline slab optimization.
elements.sent-2411-363: Otherwise, an outlined stub is created to hold the JIT code.
elements.sent-2411-364: The new stub branches to the existing stub chain head on IC miss, and the branch in the SMC region is updated to branch to the new stub.
elements.sent-2411-365: As shown in \figref{fig:ic-entry-baseline-jit-assembly , IC stubs are not functions: there is no fixed convention to communicate between the main logic and the IC stubs.}
elements.sent-2411-366: Instead, the IC stubs work directly on the machine state (e.g., which register holds what value) of the main JIT code, and may directly branch back to different places in the main JIT code.
elements.sent-2411-367: This, together with the inline slab optimization, reduces the overhead of control and data transfer to a minimum.
elements.sent-2411-368: Finally, the VM needs to manage the IC stubs.
elements.sent-2411-369: Thus, as \figref{fig:polymorphic-ic-design shows, each IC stub is accompanied by a piece of metadata, and the SlowPathData for the bytecode contains an IC site that chains all the IC metadata into a linked list.}
elements.sent-2411-370: The JIT code uses the same stack frame layout as the interpreter, and has no inter-bytecode optimizations.
elements.sent-2411-371: OSR-entry (\secref{sec:generated-vm-architecture ) is thus simply a branch to the right JIT code location.}
elements.sent-2411-372: This happens in five stages (illustrated with five colors in \figref{fig:baseline-jit-generation-pipeline ):.}
elements.sent-2411-373: Generate the bytecode implementation function and lower all Deegen API to concrete implementations.
elements.sent-2411-374: This is similar to executing steps 1--3 of the interpreter generation pipeline in \secref{sec:interpreter-generation , except that many Deegen APIs need to be lowered differently.}
elements.sent-2411-375: Identify runtime constants and their derived constant expressions in the LLVM IR, and replace them with Copy-and-Patch stencil holes (addresses of external symbols).
elements.sent-2411-376: Compile the LLVM IR to textual assembly (.s files), parse the assembly file, and run a series of analysis, transformation, and extraction passes on the textual assembly.
elements.sent-2411-377: The passes are mostly architecture-agnostic: we only need to know the control flow destinations of each instruction (i.e., whether an instruction is a branch and/or a barrier), and transformations are limited to instruction reordering: we never modify the instructions themselves.
elements.sent-2411-378: Compile the transformed textual assembly to object files and parse them to generate Copy-and-Patch stencils~\cite{copyandpatch for the main JIT code and the IC stubs.}
elements.sent-2411-379: Generate the continuation-passing style baseline JIT code generator (see \figref{fig:baseline-codegen ) that puts together all the stencils (a bytecode may have multiple stencils due to return continuations) and populates the support data (SlowPathData and others).}
elements.sent-2411-380: If IC is used, we also need to generate the IC code generator and IC management logic for the call IC and the generic IC.
elements.sent-2411-381: Due to page limits, we will focus on how the optimizations in \secref{sec:baseline-jit-design are implemented.}
elements.sent-2411-382: See \secref{appendix:life-of-the-add-bytecode for a step-by-step example of the baseline JIT generation pipeline on the Add bytecode.}
elements.sent-2411-383: For a bytecode, at execution time, everything in the bytecode and in the $IC_{state}$ (once it is created) are runtime constants.
elements.sent-2411-384: By design, Deegen has control over all of them, as Deegen is responsible for decoding the bytecode and feeding $IC_{state}$ into $\lambda_e$.
elements.sent-2411-385: So instead of generating the decoding logic to fetch the value, we simply use a magic function to identify the runtime constant.
elements.sent-2411-386: We can then find all uses of these magic functions to identify all the derived expressions that are runtime constants.
elements.sent-2411-387: For example, \textcolor{BlueBoxColor {\fboxrule=.1em\fboxsep=.1em\fbox{1 in \figref{fig:add-baseline-jit-code (defined as lhsSlot$\times$8) arises from the LLVM getelementptr node that computes the address of local lhsSlot.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-388: There is one complication: in Copy-and-Patch, runtime constants are represented by addresses of external symbols, which in x86-64 are assumed by the ABI to lie in the range $[1, 2^{31 -2^{24 )$~\cite{systemvABI .}}}
elements.sent-2411-389: This assumption must be honored for correctness: for example, the upper bound $2^{31 -2^{24}$ allows LLVM to represent the value with a 32-bit signed literal in the machine instruction (see \figref{fig:add-baseline-jit-code ), so if the expression overflows int32, the instruction would be incorrect.}}
elements.sent-2411-390: Therefore, we must statically prove that all runtime constant expressions will fit in the assumed range when evaluated at codegen time.
elements.sent-2411-391: To do this, we must know the possible range of each runtime constant.
elements.sent-2411-392: For $IC_{state}$, information from the high-level design is often needed to narrow down the range (e.g., that slot is a small non-negative integer in \figref{fig:generic-ic-api-example ), so Deegen provides the \deegenKeyword{AnnotateRange API (\figref{fig:generic-ic-api-list ) for users to annotate the possible ranges.}}}
elements.sent-2411-393: If the proven range of an expression does not fit the assumption, we have two choices: if the range length fits the assumption but the endpoints do not, we can add an adjustment value to the expression to adjust it into the assumed range, and subtract that value in LLVM IR.
elements.sent-2411-394: LLVM is smart enough so doing so will not affect final code quality.
elements.sent-2411-395: Otherwise, we give up and do the evaluation at runtime, but this case rarely happens in practice.
elements.sent-2411-396: Finally, at code generation time, we replay the expressions using the actual runtime values in the bytecode or $IC_{state}$, and patch the stencil holes accordingly: see \figref{fig:baseline-codegen for an example.}
elements.sent-2411-397: We use textual assembly transforms (\figref{fig:baseline-jit-generation-pipeline ) to implement hot-cold splitting and jump-to-fallthrough optimizations.}
elements.sent-2411-398: We parse the assembly text and use debug information as a hack to map assembly lines back to LLVM basic blocks.
elements.sent-2411-399: We then use LLVM's block frequency analysis to identify cold assembly lines, and reorder the assembly to move these cold code to a separate text section.
elements.sent-2411-400: Finally, if the fast path can branch to the next bytecode, we attempt to reorder instructions to move the branch to the last instruction and eliminate it.
elements.sent-2411-401: To implement polymorphic IC, we must generate a function that can dynamically patch itself to append a dynamic chain of parametrizable code stubs (\figref{fig:polymorphic-ic-design ).}
elements.sent-2411-402: Moreover, the code stubs work directly on the existing machine state, and they may branch back to other places in the function to continue execution (\figref{fig:ic-entry-baseline-jit-assembly ).}
elements.sent-2411-403: LLVM is clearly not designed for such use cases.
elements.sent-2411-404: After this abstraction, the function semantics becomes: we execute the logic before the IC, then execute this black box to select the IC that hits (or the IC miss handler), and finally transfer control to the logic selected by the black box.
elements.sent-2411-405: Interestingly, the GCC ASM-goto extension~\cite{gccAsmGoto provides exactly the semantics we want for the black box: an opaque piece of logic that takes in certain inputs and redirects control to somewhere else in the function (opacity is critical since LLVM must not peek into it).}
elements.sent-2411-406: In LLVM, ASM-goto is supported by a special CallBr IR node~\cite{llvmCallBrNode .}
elements.sent-2411-407: This allows us to model the IC execution semantics inside LLVM, as illustrated in \figref{fig:ic-callbr .}
elements.sent-2411-408: We use a CallBr node with an assembly payload that takes in the $IC_{key}$ and the LLVM labels for the IC miss logic ($\lambda_i$) and all different IC hit logic ($\lambda_e$).
elements.sent-2411-409: The CallBr has no output or clobber, but branches to one of the given LLVM labels.
elements.sent-2411-410: The content of the assembly payload is by design opaque to LLVM, so we use it to carry information down to the assembly level: as shown in \figref{fig:ic-callbr , it starts with a hlt so we can identify it in the assembly.}
elements.sent-2411-411: Next comes the IC check logic and the list of labels for $\lambda_i$ and each $\lambda_e$, so we know which assembly label implements each IC case.
elements.sent-2411-412: At the textual assembly level, we identify the assembly payload by locating the hlt, remove the payload, and change it into a direct branch to the IC miss slow path.
elements.sent-2411-413: We then analyze the control flow graph (CFG) of the assembly.
elements.sent-2411-414: The only hard part is that one cannot determine the possible targets of an indirect branch from the assembly.
elements.sent-2411-415: To work around this, we modified a few lines of the LLVM backend to let it dump indirect branch targets as comments next to the assembly.
elements.sent-2411-416: For example, the IC stub for $\lambda_e$\#1 in \figref{fig:cfg-analysis should contain its entry block and block B, but not block C or D as they are already available in the main logic.}
elements.sent-2411-417: Finally, for each extracted IC stub, we reorder its basic blocks to minimize branches, put them in a separate text section, and compile it into a Copy-and-Patch stencil.
elements.sent-2411-418: The inline slab optimization is just a bit more engineering.
elements.sent-2411-419: We heuristically select a good SMC region size based on the IC stub sizes.
elements.sent-2411-420: If an IC stub ends with a jump to right after the SMC region, it can be eliminated to a fallthrough, and NOPs must be padded at the end to fill the SMC region.
elements.sent-2411-421: Finally, we generate the Copy-and-Patch code generator logic that generates the IC stub, and generate the management logic that implements everything else in the polymorphic IC design.
elements.sent-2411-422: This is not trivial, but mostly engineering work, so we omit it due to space.
elements.sent-2411-423: To evaluate Deegen in practice, we used it to implement LuaJIT Remake (LJR), a standard-compliant VM for Lua 5.1.
elements.sent-2411-424: We targeted Lua 5.1 so we can have a meaningful comparision with LuaJIT~\cite{luajit , the fastest Lua VM to date (which only supports Lua 5.1).}
elements.sent-2411-425: LJR reused the parser of LuaJIT, but everything else is written from scratch.
elements.sent-2411-426: LJR supports all Lua 5.1 language features, but to focus on our research, we did not implement all Lua 5.1 standard library, and we did not yet implement GC.
elements.sent-2411-427: We stress that LJR and LuaJIT have drastically different designs.
elements.sent-2411-429: LuaJIT does not support inline caching.
elements.sent-2411-430: In contrast, IC is a central optimization in LJR.
elements.sent-2411-431: LuaJIT has an interpreter{ \textrightarrow tracing JIT architecture.}
elements.sent-2411-432: LJR is designed to have an interpreter{ \textrightarrow baseline method JIT{ \textrightarrow optimizing JIT architecture, but the optimizing JIT is future work.}}
elements.sent-2411-433: Due to the drastic design differences, merely comparing the performance of LJR and LuaJIT is insufficient to derive conclusions about Deegen.
elements.sent-2411-434: For example, LJR outperforms LuaJIT in interpreter performance, but that does not necessarily imply that the assembly generated by Deegen has better quality than what \citet{luajit21 hand-wrote, as the high-level designs are different.}
elements.sent-2411-435: In fact, the assembly of both LJR and LuaJIT are close-to-optimal under their respective designs (\secref{sec:eval-generated-code-quality ), but Deegen supports high-level optimizations (mainly IC) that lead to its better performance.}
elements.sent-2411-436: As such, we evaluate Deegen as follows: \secref{sec:eval-user-engineering-cost assesses the engineering cost of using Deegen to describe the bytecode semantics in LJR.}
elements.sent-2411-437: And \secref{sec:eval-performance-evaluation evaluates the performance of LJR on a variety of benchmarks, to provide direct evidence that Deegen can support real-world languages efficiently, and to corroborate \secref{sec:eval-generated-code-quality on the capability of Deegen by putting LJR in the context of the state of the art.}}
elements.sent-2411-438: Thanks to Deegen's \deegenKeyword{Variant API, LJR only has 42 bytecode definitions (half of LuaJIT and comparable to PUC Lua), but specializes them into 255 bytecodes for better performance.}
elements.sent-2411-439: Moreover, our semantic descriptions are in C++.
elements.sent-2411-440: Compared with LuaJIT's hand-coded x86-64 assembly, our approach has far fewer lines of code, lower engineering barrier, better maintainability, better portability, and the ability to automatically get a JIT for free.
elements.sent-2411-441: PUC Lua's interpreter has 600 fewer LLOC than LJR, but it also has few optimizations, and the line difference is small compared with the size of the whole VM (e.g., the PUC Lua parser has 1300 LLOC).
elements.sent-2411-442: As such, we conclude that Deegen allows users to implement a language at a low engineering cost similar to writing a naive interpreter.
elements.subsection-2411-009: Qualitative Assessment of Generated Code Quality
elements.sent-2411-443: As stated earlier, the performance of LJR is not sufficient to justify the quality of the code generated by Deegen.
elements.sent-2411-444: We therefore manually assessed the code quality of the interpreter and baseline JIT compiler generated by Deegen, and the quality of the JIT code generated by the baseline JIT.
elements.sent-2411-445: Many examples are already given in \figref{fig:interpreter-add-assembly,fig:interpreter-getbyid-assembly,fig:baseline-codegen,fig:add-baseline-jit-code,fig:getbyid-baseline-jit-assembly,fig:ic-entry-baseline-jit-assembly , but interested readers may also find the full disassembly of all LJR bytecodes in the artifact for their own assessment.}
elements.sent-2411-446: We used our best judgement, reference code from state-of-the-art VMs, and the uiCA latency predictor~\cite{uica to look for improvements in the Deegen-generated code.}
elements.sent-2411-447: For the interpreter, we used LuaJIT and JavaScriptCore's hand-coded-in-assembly interpreters for reference.
elements.sent-2411-448: For the JIT code, we used JavaScriptCore's baseline JIT MacroAssembler logic for reference.
elements.sent-2411-449: For the baseline JIT compiler, we are unable to find any improvement.
elements.sent-2411-450: This is not surprising, since the logic is branchless by design and modern compilers like LLVM excel at branchless code.
elements.sent-2411-451: For the interpreter and the JIT code, we are unable to find improvements in the majority of them.
elements.sent-2411-452: However, for a few complex bytecodes, we are able to identify four areas of minor improvements:.
elements.sent-2411-453: Stack operations (e.g., push, rsp adjustments) that can be sinked to the slow path.
elements.sent-2411-454: Unwanted hoisting of simple instructions (mainly mov between registers) to the fast path.
elements.sent-2411-455: Sub-optimal tail duplication and tail merge decisions when a lot of tail blocks exist.
elements.sent-2411-456: Register shuffling to fulfill C calling convention when making a runtime call.
elements.sent-2411-457: The former three can be attributed to minor LLVM deficiencies.
elements.sent-2411-458: The last is an inherent limitation of calling conventions, but can be fixed by introducing flexible calling conventions to LLVM.
elements.sent-2411-459: Nevertheless, most of the code is optimal to our eyes.
elements.sent-2411-460: Additionally, all four issues above can be characterized as having one simple extra instruction (e.g., push, mov, jmp).
elements.sent-2411-461: Modern CPUs excel at instruction-level parallelism, so these issues are unlikely to cause noticeable slowdown.
elements.sent-2411-462: As such, we claim that the code quality of the interpreter and baseline JIT generated by Deegen rivals existing state-of-the-art interpreters and baseline JITs hand-written in assembly by experts.
elements.sent-2411-463: We evaluate LuaJIT Remake, LuaJIT 2.1~\cite{luajit21 , and PUC Lua~\cite{puclua51 on 44 benchmarks from AWFY~\cite{awfy , CLBG~\cite{clbg , LuaJIT-Bench~\cite{luajitbench and LuaBench~\cite{luabench .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-464: Since GC is not implemented in LJR, we turned off GC in LuaJIT and PUC Lua as well.
elements.sent-2411-465: Due to the large design difference between LJR and LuaJIT, we also labeled the benchmarks based on their main workload types, to shed light on where the performance differences come from.
elements.sent-2411-466: We ran all the experiments on a laptop with an Intel i7-12700H CPU with turbo boost on.
elements.sent-2411-468: All experiments are single-threaded, and task-pinned to a fixed P-core to remove noise due to core discrepancy and OS scheduling.
elements.sent-2411-469: We report the average across three runs.
elements.sent-2411-470: On average, LJR's interpreter outperforms LuaJIT's interpreter by 31\%, and outperforms PUC Lua by 179\%.
elements.sent-2411-471: LuaJIT has a different string implementation from the rest, leading to 3 cases where it outperforms LJR and one case (life) where its performance plummets.
elements.paragraph-2411-028: Baseline JIT Startup Delay and Memory Cost
elements.sent-2411-472: The top priority of the baseline JIT is to compile fast.
elements.sent-2411-473: Averaged across the 44 benchmarks, the baseline JIT in LJR can compile 19.1 million Lua bytecodes per second, generating machine code at 1.62GiB/s.
elements.sent-2411-474: As such, the startup delay is practically negligible.
elements.sent-2411-475: On average, each bytecode is translated to 91 bytes of machine code.
elements.sent-2411-476: Based on our assessment of the JIT code disassembly (\secref{sec:eval-generated-code-quality ), the room for code size improvement exists but is small.}
elements.figure-2411-006: \begin{minipage}{\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{eval-lua.eps.converted.pdf} \end{minipage} \begin{minipage}{\textwidth} \begin{minipage}{0.005\textwidth}\end{minipage} \begin{minipage}{0.49\textwidth} \caption{Performance comparison between LJR, LuaJIT and PUC Lua (interpreter-only mode).} \label{fig:interpreter-perf-comparison} \end{minipage} \begin{minipage}{0.06\textwidth}\end{minipage} \begin{minipage}{0.45\textwidth} \caption{Performance comparison between LJR, LuaJIT and PUC Lua (JIT-enabled mode).} \label{fig:jit-perf-comparison} \end{minipage} \end{minipage}
elements.sent-2411-477: Generating an optimizing JIT is future work.
elements.sent-2411-478: Thus, LJR currently only has a baseline JIT, which by design prioritizes compilation speed over generated JIT code quality, and is not designed to compete with an optimizing JIT (such as the tracing JIT in LuaJIT) in terms of peak throughput.
elements.sent-2411-479: Despite not being an apple-to-apple comparison, \figref{fig:jit-perf-comparison shows the performance of LJR's baseline JIT and LuaJIT's optimizing tracing JIT, normalized against the performance of the PUC Lua interpreter.}
elements.sent-2411-480: LJR is on average 33\% slower than LuaJIT, and 360\% faster than PUC Lua.
elements.sent-2411-481: As one can see, the majority of the cases where LJR loses to LuaJIT are number-crunching workloads (loops of mathematical expressions), which is expected due to our lack of the optimizing JIT tier.
elements.sent-2411-482: Nevertheless, the only high-level optimization in our baseline JIT, inline caching, is not supported by LuaJIT.
elements.sent-2411-483: As a result, LJR is already faster than LuaJIT on 13 of the 44 benchmarks.
elements.sent-2411-484: We compare Deegen with work in four areas: interpreter generators, generic JIT compilers via meta-tracing and the first Futamura projection, LLVM-based JIT compilers, and compiler generators.
elements.sent-2411-485: Interpreter generators can be traced at least back to \citet{10.1145/582153.582178 , who generated a Pascal interpreter from formal denotational language semantics, though it was too slow to use in practice.}
elements.sent-2411-486: They take bytecode implementations in a light DSL on top of C and support optimizations such as direct-threading, bytecode specialization, superinstruction, top-of-stack caching, and code replication.
elements.sent-2411-487: TruffleDSL~\cite{truffleDSL targets dynamic languages by generating a type-based self-optimizing AST interpreter~\cite{truffleSelfOptimizingInterpreter , similar to dynamic quickening~\cite{10.1145/1899661.1869633 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-488: However, the overhead of their AST interpreter proved to be high, so they are developing a new OperationDSL~\cite{operationdsl, operationdslgithub to generate bytecode interpreters.}
elements.sent-2411-489: The most relevant work to Deegen is TruffleDSL (and its planned successor OperationDSL), as both aim to generate highly optimized interpreters for dynamic languages.
elements.sent-2411-490: The main difference is real-world performance.
elements.sent-2411-491: Deegen's interpreter significantly outperforms LuaJIT's highly-optimized hand-coded-in-assembly interpreter.
elements.sent-2411-492: In contrast, as reported by \citet{Marr2022GraalWorkshop , Truffle's interpreters are $\sim$10x slower than even the less optimized interpreters like CPython or CRuby.}
elements.sent-2411-493: There are two prior approaches to get a JIT-capable VM without writing a JIT: meta-tracing~\cite{BolzTereick2009TracingTM and the first Futamura projection~\cite{futamuraPaper .}}
elements.sent-2411-494: Both approaches lead to a generic JIT compiler that can work on any guest language specification.
elements.sent-2411-495: Meta-tracing, pioneered by PyPy~\cite{BolzTereick2009TracingTM and followed by GVMT~\cite{gvmt and YK~\cite{ykjit , traces the execution of an interpreter written in a traceable language (in contrast to traditional tracing that traces the bytecodes in the user program).}}}
elements.sent-2411-496: The meta-tracer is thus a generic tracing-JIT that works for any interpreter written in the traceable language.
elements.sent-2411-497: PyPy traces RPython code, while GVMT and YK trace C code.
elements.sent-2411-498: Meta-tracing has good user ergonomics~\cite{convergemetatracing and can naturally desugar complex language constructs.}
elements.sent-2411-499: Nevertheless, its interpreter suffers from high tracing overhead (100$\times$-200$\times$ when tracing a loop~\cite{ykhalmet ), it lacks a baseline JIT (there are proposals~\cite{dls2020metatracing, izawa2022twolevel , but their performance and maturity are still not enough for real-world use~\cite{pypypersonalcommunication ), the inherently-large trace (compared with traditional tracing) increases JIT startup delay~\cite{convergemetatracing , and the tracing approach can hit degenerative cases on some workloads~\cite{mozzilaAbandonTracingJit .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-500: The first Futamura projection, theorized by \citet{futamuraPaper , is a generic method-JIT that works by partially evaluating an interpreter on an interpreted program at runtime.}
elements.sent-2411-501: Work to practicalize this idea dates back to ~\cite{10.1145/258366.258408, 10.1023/A:1010078412711 , but Truffle~\cite{trufflePaper is the first to practicalize it for dynamic languages.}}
elements.sent-2411-502: While Truffle has a high optimizing JIT peak throughput, this comes at the cost of poor interpreter performance, high interpreter memory footprint, lack of a true baseline JIT tier, and high JIT startup delay (\secref{sec:introduction ).}
elements.sent-2411-503: Other projects in building a generic JIT through the first Futamura projection include LMS~\cite{10.1145/1868294.1868314 and HolyJIT~\cite{holyjit , which work by partial-evaluating the Scala and Rust AST respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-504: Deegen does not use a generic JIT.
elements.sent-2411-505: Instead, Deegen is a compiler generator that statically generates specialized JITs (and interpreters).
elements.sent-2411-506: This allows us to generate the most suitable implementation for each VM tier, thus fully avoiding the drawbacks of the prior approaches above.
elements.sent-2411-507: LLVM's powerful code-generation infrastructure made it a popular choice to build JIT compilers, with wide success in fields such as databases~\cite{neumann2011, pelotonQueryCompilation, postgresJit, memsql and scientific computing languages~\cite{numba, julia .}}
elements.sent-2411-508: There were also many attempts to use LLVM in dynamic language VMs~\cite{unladenswallow,rubinius,llvmlua,sulong,jscftl , but none have mainstream adoption today.}
elements.sent-2411-509: LLVM's high startup delay and lack of support for dynamic-language-specific optimizations (e.g., inline caching) are considered as the main reasons~\cite{unladenswallowretrospective, jscb3backend .}
elements.sent-2411-510: Deegen does not use LLVM as a JIT.
elements.sent-2411-511: Instead, Deegen uses LLVM to statically generate the JIT.
elements.sent-2411-512: This renders LLVM's high compilation cost irrelevant, and allows us to support domain-specific optimizations via assembly transforms, thus giving us LLVM's power without its drawbacks.
elements.sent-2411-513: Most of the prior work on compiler generators, e.g., Yacc~\cite{yacc , focus on parser generation.}
elements.sent-2411-514: Research on generating full compiler backends is surprisingly rare.
elements.sent-2411-515: The most recent work on full compiler generation is the PQCC project~\cite{pqcc, pqccretrospective in the early 1980s.}
elements.sent-2411-516: PQCC focuses on low-level optimizations of C-like languages, and languages of higher abstraction levels (including dynamic languages) are explicitly not considered.
elements.sent-2411-517: To the best of our knowledge, Deegen is the first work after 40 years of silence in full compiler backend generation research.
elements.sent-2411-518: We introduced Deegen, a practical and novel compiler generator for building high-performance dynamic language VMs at low engineering cost.
elements.sent-2411-519: We empirically demonstrated Deegen's capabilities via LuaJIT Remake, a standard-compliant Lua 5.1 VM powered by Deegen.
elements.sent-2411-520: There are still numerous opportunities to further improve Deegen.
elements.sent-2411-521: The top priority is clearly the third-tier optimizing JIT, but just to name a few more: optimizations around built-in integer types to efficiently support Lua 5.3 and Python; a dedicated compiler thread to hide JIT startup delay; persistent JIT code caching to completely remove JIT cost across runs; portability to the ARM architecture; optimizations on library functions; a better story for C-bindings and FFI, the GC, multi-threading, perhaps even a fourth-tier heavyweight optimizing JIT in the far future.
elements.sent-2411-522: With all of this future work, we envision Deegen to eventually become the infrastructure for a wide range of dynamic languages, bringing them state-of-the-art performance at low engineering cost.
elements.sent-2411-523: We are deeply grateful to Saam Barati for spending countless hours of his after-work time teaching us everything we want to know about JavaScriptCore.
elements.sent-2411-524: Without his help, this project would not have reached its current state.
elements.sent-2411-525: We would also like to thank all individuals who provided helpful discussions, comments, and references throughout the course of the project.
elements.sent-2411-526: This work was supported by the Stanford Agile Hardware Center.

elements.section-2411-011: The Life of the Add Bytecode
elements.sent-2411-771: This appendix elaborates step-by-step how Deegen lowers the C++ execution semantics of LuaJIT Remake's Add bytecode to the baseline JIT code generator illustrated in \figref{fig:baseline-codegen .}
elements.sent-2411-772: The rest of the section will use the Add bytecode as example to explain what happens in each pass of the \figref{fig:baseline-jit-generation-pipeline pipeline.}
elements.sent-2411-773: Below is the real C++ implementation of the semantics for arithmetic bytecodes in LJR.
elements.sent-2411-774: The code above defines only one variant of the Add bytecode: the one that adds a local variable to another local variable (for brevity, we use Add\_0 to refer to this bytecode variant).
elements.sent-2411-775: Throughout this section, we will use Add\_0 as our example.
elements.sent-2411-776: The first step of the pipeline is to compile the C++ semantics to LLVM IR.
elements.sent-2411-777: Importantly, all LLVM passes are disabled (via the -Xclang -disable-llvm-passes flag), so the LLVM IR is the raw IR generated by the Clang frontend, and even the functions marked as always\_inline are not inlined.
